coredata.dc:title: Microwave formation and photoluminescence mechanisms of multi-states nitrogen doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: He, Guili
Shu, Mengjun
Yang, Zhi
Ma, Yujie
Huang, Da
Xu, Shusheng
Wang, Yanfang
Hu, Nantao
Zhang, Yafei
Xu, Lin
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention in the material field because of their remarkable performance in various aspects. Therefore, the exploration of complex and variable photoluminescence mechanisms shows great significance. Herein, we present a systematic study on the correlation between the formation process and photoluminescence mechanisms through the characterization and analysis of three states of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) obtained by microwave irradiation. At low temperature of 160°C, the small organic molecule polymer nanodots whose photoluminescence center is molecule state are obtained with superior quantum yield of about 51.61%. Increasing the reaction temperature up to 200°C, the intermediate transition products named carbon nanodots begin to appear. Prolonging the holding time, the typical carbon quantum dots with a special stable optical properties are finally generated, and their most photoluminescence arises from the carbon cores which are gained through the polymerization, dehydration, carbonation of organic fluorescent molecules. Furthermore, N-CDs have been applied in metal ions detection as well as animal and plant cell fluorescence imaging owing to their excellent water solubility and low cytotoxicity. Our exploration provides the theoretical basis for synthesis of CDs with different properties and purposes. In the near future, more high-quality CDs will be developed in order to better benefit the various fields of mankind.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Microwave irradiation
Photoluminescence mechanisms
Metal ions detection
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) Photoluminescence emission spectra of N-CDs160-1, N-CDs200-1 and N-CDs200-2, respectively; (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs.
TEM images and the corresponding particle size distribution of (a) and (b) N-CDs160-1, (c) and (d) N-CDs200-1, (e) and (f) N-CDs200-2.
(a) Raman spectra and (b) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs.
(a) XPS scanning spectra of N-CDs. XPS high resolution survey scan of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s region of N-CDs.
A Schematic diagram of formation process of N-CDs.

(a) Viability of BT-474 cells incubation with N-CDs of different concentrations; confocal fluorescence microphotographs of N-CDs labeled BT-474 cells under (b) bright field; (c) 405nm excitation; (d) the merged image of (b) and (c).
Cellular imaging of stem cross section of celery under (a) bright field; (b) 405nm excitation; (c) the merged image of (a) and (b).

head.ce:title: Microwave formation and photoluminescence mechanisms of multi-states nitrogen doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Thin Film and Microfabrication of Ministry of Education, Department of Micro/Nano Electronics, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Thin Film and Microfabrication of Ministry of Education
School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, Institute for Personalized Medicine, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ophthalmology, Shanghai General Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Shanghai 200240, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory for Thin Film and Microfabrication of Ministry of Education, Department of Micro/Nano Electronics, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention in the material field because of their remarkable performance in various aspects. Therefore, the exploration of complex and variable photoluminescence mechanisms shows great significance. Herein, we present a systematic study on the correlation between the formation process and photoluminescence mechanisms through the characterization and analysis of three states of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) obtained by microwave irradiation. At low temperature of 160°C, the small organic molecule polymer nanodots whose photoluminescence center is molecule state are obtained with superior quantum yield of about 51.61%. Increasing the reaction temperature up to 200°C, the intermediate transition products named carbon nanodots begin to appear. Prolonging the holding time, the typical carbon quantum dots with a special stable optical properties are finally generated, and their most photoluminescence arises from the carbon cores which are gained through the polymerization, dehydration, carbonation of organic fluorescent molecules. Furthermore, N-CDs have been applied in metal ions detection as well as animal and plant cell fluorescence imaging owing to their excellent water solubility and low cytotoxicity. Our exploration provides the theoretical basis for synthesis of CDs with different properties and purposes. In the near future, more high-quality CDs will be developed in order to better benefit the various fields of mankind.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a kind of zero-dimensional nanoparticles, exhibit uniform particle sizes, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, chemical inertness, easy functional modification, and adjustable fluorescence. Hence they hold a great promise in energy conversion and storage, bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, diagnostics and nanocomposite, etc. [1–3]. The chemical methods commonly used in the preparation of CDs include electrochemical method [4,5], solvothermal method [6,7], laser etching [8,9], microwave method [10–12], and so on. Among these methods, the microwave method is more favored because of its short reaction time, less energy consumption, easy operation, and environmental friendliness [13]. The properties of the resultant CDs are different due to the various synthetic methods. Many researchers are committed to investigating the formation process and the photoluminescence mechanisms of CDs. Based on the chemical structure, CDs can be simply divided into three categories: polymer dots (PDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [14,15]. Usually, PDs and a part of CNDs are amorphous, while the other CNDs and CQDs usually have obvious lattice. These three kinds of CDs possess multi-level photoluminescence mechanisms, which, in essence, are due to their imparity photoluminescent centers. The photoluminescence center of PDs is formed solely by an organic fluorophore calling the molecule state, while CNDs are attributed to the synergetic hybridization of the chemical groups and the carbon core which named the surface state. For CQDs, the carbon core is the dominant factor of their photoluminescence induced by the quantum confinement effect of perfect carbon crystals [16]. Although much progress has been made in the theoretical studies of CDs synthesis pathway and photoluminescence mechanisms, more in-depth research is eager for comprehensive and detailed analysis of CDs so that high-quality CDs can be synthesized on a large scale for various practical applications.

It can be drawn from the study of CDs in recent years that the quantum yield (QY) of the bare CDs is relatively low (usually less than 10%), which severely limits the application of CDs as well as hinders the development of the emerging carbon nanomaterials [17]. Therefore, numerous methods have been put forward to solve this problem, including the doping of nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur and other heteroatoms, or surface modification using organic molecules. At present, a large number of researches indicate that nitrogen doping is one of the most efficient and simplest ways to improve the QY of CDs. Naturally, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) have become the new favorite in the field of carbon nanomaterials.

In this work, high-quality N-CDs were successfully prepared by using acetylacetone and ammonia as initial reactants, in which microwave irradiation was applied to provide high temperature and high pressure environment. By adjusting the reaction parameters such as reactant ratio, reaction temperature, and soaking time, N-CDs with different physicochemical and optical properties were finally obtained. Through the analysis of the data received by a series of characterization, the inference was proposed to explain the formation process of N-CDs which could be simply described as intramolecular and/or intermolecular dehydration, polymerization and carbide nucleation, at the same time the correlation between the compositions of N-CDs and the photoluminescence mechanisms was further explored. In addition, the as-prepared N-CDs with high QY and minimal toxicity were used in ion detection and animal and plant cell fluorescence imaging.

Ammonia solution (NH3·H2O) purchased from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Acetylacetone, silver nitrate (AgNO3), aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O), calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2·4H2O), cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), iron (III) chloride (FeCl3), sodium chloride (NaCl), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), lead (II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2) were all of analytical reagent grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), a 0.25% (w/v) trypsin/0.03% (w/v) ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) solution and Roswell Park Memorial Institude (RPMI)-1640 medium were purchased from Gibco Life Technologies (Shanghai, China). The CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay Kit was purchased from Promega (Beijing, China). The phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was purchased from Wuhan Boster Biological Technology Co. Ltd (Wuhan, China). BT-474 cells were purchased from American type culture collection, USA. Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm was used for all experiments.

Acetylacetone and NH3·H2O with a certain volume ratio were mixed and put into a bottle, followed by adding 5mL of deionized water. A single mode microwave reaction apparatus bought from PreeKem Science and Technology Development Company (Shanghai, China) was applied to provide high temperature and pressure. Reactants were heated up to different temperature like 160°C, 180°C and 200°C in a period of 5min, and dwelled at this temperature for 10 or 30min. N-CDs with various QY were eventually obtained. In this work, we got a variety of N-CDs through adoption of different heating temperatures and holding times.

To detect metal ions, a series of aqueous solutions including Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ (a calculated amount of ions of 5mM) were added into N-CDs solution (0.01mM) without further purification, respectively.

The breast cancer cells (BT-474) with a density of 1×104 cells/well were put into a 96-well plate. Firstly, the cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS for 24h at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2-contained atmosphere. Next, the N-CDs with different concentrations were added to incubate for another 24h. Meanwhile, cells incubated without N-CDs were set as blank control group. Subsequently, the medium containing N-CDs was removed. The viability of these cells was measured using the CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay kit according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The data were analyzed by Graphpad PRISM 5 software.

The BT-474 cells were put into a 8-well plate and incubated in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 overnight, subsequently added the N-CDs to make the concentration of 1000μg/mL, and then incubated for another 6h. The cells were washed with PBS for three times. Finally, the cells were observed under confocal laser scanning microscope. In the experiments of plant cell fluorescence imaging, the celery root was washed clearly and inserted into 1000μg/mL N-CDs solution. After cultured for overnight, the cross section of the celery stem was observed under confocal laser scanning microscope.

Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence emission (PLE) spectra were recorded using a fluorescent spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired by a UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, Perkin-Elmer, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using a steady-state & time-resolved fluorescence spectrofluorometer (QM/TM/IM, PTI, USA). The morphologies of the samples were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). Raman spectrum was performed using a dispersive Raman microscope (Bruker Senterra, Germany) with a laser at a wavelength of 633nm. Fourier transformation infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded on a spectrometer (VERTEX 70, Bruker, Germany) at room temperature with deuterated L-alanine-doped triglycine sulfate as a detector. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were acquired using a spectrometer (Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Japan) with a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486.6eV). Fluorescence microscope images were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Nikon A1si, Japan).

In this work, the QY was measured using quinine sulfate (54.6% in 0.1M sulfuric acid) as a reference under 340nm UV light excitation [10]. In order to acquire N-CDs with higher QY, the volume ratio of initial reactants (acetylacetone:NH3·H2O) and reaction temperature were adjusted to find the optimum conditions. The volume ratio values of 2:1, 1:1, 2:3 and 1:2 under different reaction temperatures of 160°C, 180°C and 200°C were selected, respectively. The photos of as-prepared N-CDs (the product without post-treatment) are shown in Fig. S1, and the corresponding QY is displayed in Table S1. As the reaction temperature increases, the color of the N-CDs solution becomes darker and the QY declines, which can be simply attributed to the consumption of fluorescent organic molecules due to carbonization. In addition, the QY also reduces with the decrease of volume ratio value. Moreover, excess NH3·H2O will make the reaction pressure overrun, resulting in the termination of the reaction sequence. In general, both the volume ratio and RT value can greatly influence the QY of N-CDs. The highest QY of 51.61% is acquired when the volume ratio is 1:2 with the reaction temperature at 160°C, which is actually higher than the most previous reported QYs of carbon-based nanomaterials [18]. So the volume ratio value is fixed at 1:2 to focus on the influence of temperature on the QY as well as the formation process and photoluminescence mechanisms of N-CDs.

Photoluminescence is the most important feature of N-CDs, which not only determines the scope of application of N-CDs, but also an important basis for inferring the photoluminescence process. N-CDs160-1 stand for N-CDs which were obtained by keeping reaction at 160°C for 10min, and their emission spectrum is displayed in Fig. 1 a. With the excitation wavelength increased from 310 to 380nm, the position of emission peak always stays at 420nm, but the fluorescent intensity is changed. When N-CDs160-1 are excited at 340nm, the strongest fluorescent is got, which agrees with the result revealed by PLE spectrum of N-CDs160-1 in Fig. S2. When the insulating time is prolonged to 30min and 1h, N-CDs160-2 and N-CDs160-3 are obtained, respectively, and the corresponding PLE and PL spectra are shown in Fig. S3. They show the similar excitation-emission independent properties as N-CDs160-1, suggesting that they may possess the same photoluminescence mechanisms. In order to further explore the influence of temperature on CDs synthesis, N-CDs200-1 and N-CDs200-2 were acquired by reacting at 200°C for 10min and 30min, respectively. As seen in Fig. S2, compared with N-CDs160-1, they both show a striking difference in excitation spectra with the maximum excitation peaks at 345 and 360nm, respectively. At the same time, their PL spectra also demonstrate distinctive properties. From PL spectra of N-CDs200-2 (Fig. 1c), the emission peak red shift from 420 to 480nm with the excitation wavelength increases from 320 to 410nm, exhibiting the distinct excitation-emission dependence. The PL spectra of N-CDs200-1 (Fig. 1b) seem to suggest that N-CDs200-1 is the intermediate state of N-CDs160-1 and N-CDs200-2, and the peak position has not changed until the excitation wavelength increased to 370nm. Thus an inference can be mentioned that N-CDs160-1, N-CDs200-1 and N-CDs200-2 may be three different types of N-CDs with different photoluminescence mechanisms.

In order to further determine the distinction in the optical properties of three kinds of N-CDs, the UV–vis spectra of the aqueous dispersion liquid of N-CDs are characterized and demonstrated in Fig. 1d. A maximum peak at 300nm is ascribed to the n–π* transition of CO bond or CN bond, whereas a shoulder at 250nm is attributed to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bond, revealing that there might be benzene rings in N-CDs [19]. The absorbance ratios of peaks at 250 and 300nm grow from N-CDs160-1 to N-CDs200-2, indicating more carbon cores are generated with the increase of reaction temperature and time. Based on the recent literatures [20–22], we can draw a conclusion that N-CDs160-1 is a kind of small organic molecule clusters, while N-CDs200-2 are mainly composed of carbon core, with little oxygen/nitrogen-based groups decorated on the surfaces. N-CDs200-1 are transitional products of polymer clusters to carbon core. Fig. S4 is the optical photos of the aqueous solution of N-CDs. Higher temperatures and longer reaction time make the color of N-CDs solutions become darker under daylight (Fig. S4a). When exposed to 365nm UV light, N-CDs160-1 and N-CDs200-1 emit blue fluorescence in aqueous solution, but the PL intensity of N-CDs160-1 is much stronger than that of N-CDs200-1. Varying from the above two kinds of N-CDs, N-CDs200-2 show green fluorescence, indicate that these three kinds of N-CDs may possess differences in structure and photoluminescence. In Fig. S4c, a beam of green light (520nm) is employed to illuminate the N-CDs aqueous solution to observe the Tyndall effect, which directly proves the excellent dispersibility of the prepared N-CDs [23].

The fluorescence decay profile of N-CDs (as shown in Fig. S5) is monitored under wavelength of 340nm excitation and 410nm emission representing as a single exponential decay. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of N-CDs160-1, N-CDs200-1 and N-CDs200-2 are ca. 3.66, 8.55 and 10.12 ns, respectively. Typically, the fluorescence lifetimes of organic dyes do not exceed 5ns in visible light, but QDs usually have the comparatively longer lifetimes from five to even hundreds of nanoseconds [24]. By comparing the fluorescence lifetime, the conjecture is once again verified that N-CDs160-1 is a fluorescent group similar to the fluorescent dye, but N-CDs200-2 may be perceived as a kind of CQDs.

In order to further explore the differences in structure and composition of the prepared N-CDs, a series of characterizations like TEM, Raman, FT-IR and XPS were carried out. The morphology and the corresponding particle size distributions of N-CDs are characterized by TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . N-CDs160-1 have the largest size of about 4.31nm because of the looser structure of polymer clusters (Fig. 2a and b). Raising the temperature to 200°C, N-CDs200-1 are generated with the smallest diameter of about 1.05nm (Fig. 2c and d), which may due to a little number of carbon cores gradually formed by dehydration and carbonization. The carbon cores grow bigger by prolonging the reaction time, and the particle sizes of N-CDs200-2 increase to about 2.24nm (Fig. 2e and f). There are no crystal lattices in Fig. 2a and c indicating that N-CDs160-1 and N-CDs200-1 are amorphous carbon particles. But an obvious lattice fringe of 0.24nm is observed in HR-TEM of N-CDs200-2 (Fig. 2e inset), which corresponds to the (020) crystal phase of graphite. The existence of crystal lattice proves that the carbon cores with graphene like structure are exactly generated in N-CDs200-2, which is consistent with UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1d).

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to analysis the surface functional groups on N-CDs. After adding ammonia to acetyl acetone, a kind of white crystal was generated rapidly, and fully dissolved in water by ultrasonic. This solution is the precursor of N-CDs, and its FT-IR spectrum is demonstrated in Fig. S6. Two main absorption bands at around 3354 and 3168cm−1 are related to OH and NH stretching vibration, respectively. Peaks at 1619 and 1400cm−1 are characteristic absorption of primary amide, the bending vibration of the amine II NH bond is at 1619cm−1, and the stretch vibration of the amine III CN bond is at 1400cm−1. The characteristic peaks of secondary amide appear at 1534 and 1288cm−1, which are corresponding to the bending vibration of the amine II CNH bond and the stretch vibration of the amine III CN bond, respectively. It can be estimated that this precursor is small organic molecules rich in amide groups. Fig. 3b is the FT-IR spectra of the products. The characteristic peaks of three samples are almost at the same positions with different relative intensity except for the peak at 1090cm−1 of N-CDs200-2, which represents the asymmetric stretching vibrations of COC. This band begins to appear at 200°C, and increases significantly with longer reaction time, indicating the most intermolecular dehydration and polymerization were taken place.

In order to further investigate the elemental composition of N-CDs, XPS analysis was carried out. The survey spectra contain three peaks at around 286, 399, and 531eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, and the relative contents of each component are displayed in Fig. 4 a. Oxygen content of N-CDs200-1 has an obvious upgrade, but nitrogen content declines, which may be attributed to amide groups’ hydrolysis under the high temperature. At the same time, this result gives a great explanation why N-CDs200-1 process relatively higher disorder. Four different chemical environments are revealed in the high resolution scan of the C1s region (Fig. 4b), and they are sequentially corresponding to CC/CH, CN, CO/COC and CO groups, respectively. Two peaks are included in the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4c), which are assigned to CN and NH groups. The high resolution O1s spectrum can be divided into two peaks, which are attributed to the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively (Fig. 4d). The XPS peaks of N-CDs200-2 show visible red-shift compared with the others, which may be ascribed to different structures caused by gradually generated carbon core. The results of XPS spectra are in consistent with that of FT-IR spectra, together suggesting the as-prepared N-CDs are partially oxidized, and these functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of N-CDs in aqueous system, which promises them an extensive prospect for applications in biochemical applications.

Next, we investigated the fluorescence stability of these three N-CDs in aqueous solution under extreme environments like UV irradiation and strong acid and alkali. In Fig. S7a, an 8W LED light source with an emission wavelength of 365nm is used to continuously irradiate the aqueous solution of N-CDs. After only 10min of excitation, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs160-1 decreases to 2.56%. This high photobleaching efficiency is very similar to that of the organic fluorescent dyes [23]. Thus, it is confirmed that N-CDs160-1 are indeed an organic fluorescent small molecule clusters. In contrast, the PL intensity of N-CDs200-2 remains 65.89% after 60min of continuous excitation, which is far superior to that of N-CDs160-1. The result indicates two extreme photoluminescence mechanisms between N-CDs160-1and N-CDs200-2. N-CDs200-2 is more inclined to quantum dots luminescence. In Fig. S7b, the PL intensity of the N-CDs under different pH values is demonstrated. All N-CDs solution can maintain high fluorescence intensities even under extremely acidic conditions, but when the solution is slightly alkaline, the fluorescence intensity will be dramatically reduced. Compared to the other two N-CDs, N-CDs160-1 alkali resistance is obviously slightly stronger, which attributes to a mass of amide groups bonding on the surface. The effects of pH can be weakened by extensive protonation-deprotonation of the amide group [5].

The surface of N-CDs is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups which can be complexed with metal ions, so N-CDs are often used to selectively detect specific metal ions [26–28]. Fig. S8 is the histogram showing PL intensity changes of N-CDs in the presence of different metal ions. Obviously, Fe3+ shows the strongest fluorescence quenching effect on N-CDs among those chosen metal ions, which probably due to that Fe3+ can catalyze the amide group to produce transamination. N-CDs160-1 are more suitable for the detection of Fe3+ than CDs200-2 in terms of specific selectivity as well as responsive sensitivity, which may be attributed to the more abundant amide groups on the surface of N-CDs160-1.

Herein, we give a brief description of the synthetic process of N-CDs and infer the association between the constituent structure and the photoluminescence mechanisms of N-CDs based on the above characterization results, and a schematic diagram is displayed in Fig. 5 . At room temperature, acetylacetone and ammonia are mixed by sonication and then acylated to form amide-rich organic precursors. Then the cross-linked molecular clusters (N-CDs160-1) are generated through intermolecular and intramolecular dehydration after the organic precursors being heated to 160°C and incubated for 10min. These organic molecules clusters possess a very strong blue fluorescence since they are rich in amide bonds. Their optical properties including excitation-emission dependence, high photobleaching efficiency and short fluorescence lifetime are very similar to organic fluorescent dyes, so N-CDs160-1 is a kind of the molecular state whose fluorescence center is based on the organic molecules. When the reaction temperature is raised to 200°C, a portion of the amide bonds are hydrolyzed in the alkaline environment and a small part of the organic groups begin to gradually carbonize to form carbon core. Therefore, N-CDs200-1 can be regarded as a carbon core with surface modified with a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs200-1 decreases because some fluorescent groups are consumed in carbonized process. While, most fluorescence of N-CDs200-1 is still derived from organic fluorescent groups, and only a small part is attributed to the synergy between the carbon core and the oxygen-containing groups on the surface, which is classified as surface state luminescence. As the heating time is prolonged, more and more fluorophores are carbonized into carbon core, leading N-CDs200-2 formed with much diminished PL intensity and QY. In addition to their crystal lattice, N-CDs200-2 possess a completely different optical property from that of N-CDs160-1, they can be classified as quantum dots luminescencewith carbon core as the main fluorescence center [29–31]. In a word, the formation process of N-CDs can be summarized of three steps as polymerization, dehydration and carbonization. The different reaction temperature and heating time have important influences on the formation and luminescence of N-CDs.

It has been mentioned that water-soluble fluorescent N-CDs could be used as cell imaging probe due to their low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [32,33]. In this work, MTS assays were carried out to evaluate the toxicity of as prepared N-CDs to BT-474 cells [34]. Fig. 6 a depicts the viability of BT-474 cells incubated with N-CDs of various concentrations. It can be can clearly observed through the histogram that when the concentration of N-CDs is less than 500μg/mL, the effect on cell activity is almost negligible, but when the concentration increases up to 1000μg/mL, the cell activity is significantly reduced. Fortunately, the decreases are still in an acceptable range. The survival rate of BT-474 cells incubation with N-CDs200-2 reduces to 60%, while the cells still maintains more than 80% survival rate in the presence of N-CDs160-1 and N-CDs200-1, implying their low (or almost no) toxicity. Considering N-CDs160-1 possesses a very high fluorescent quantum yield (51.61%) and a relatively low cytotoxicity, They are indeed an excellent fluorescent imaging probe. BT-474 cells were incubated with N-CDs160-1 at 1000μg/mL for 6h, and observed under laser scanning confocal microscope to evaluate the effect fluorescence imaging. The morphology of the as-treated cells under the bright field is integrity and health (shown in Fig. 6b), indicating that CDs160-1 has good cytocompatibility and low toxicity. Fig. 6c is shown under 405nm excitation, the as-treated cells exhibit clearly visible fluorescence. Fig. 6d is the merged image of b and c.

The celery was inserted into a 1000μg/mL aqueous solution of N-CDs160-1 for one night to verify that N-CDs160-1 can also be used to label plant cells, and the entire plant show obvious blue light under UV light. The morphology of stem section under laser confocal microscopy is displayed in Fig. 7 . It can be clearly observed that N-CDs160-1 have been successfully assimilated into plant cells, and most of them accumulate in the vessels which are responsible for transporting water and nutrients in plants. These results prove that as-prepared N-CDs are potential fluorescent dye and marker both in animal and plant cells.

In this work, the correlations between the intrinsic structure and the photoluminescence mechanisms of as-prepared N-CDs were in-depth studied through a series of characterization analysis. Under low temperature conditions (160°C), it is easier to obtain organic fluorescent molecules with high QY of 51.61%, and they had been successfully used in biological cell imaging since of nearly nontoxic. Under high temperature (200°C), N-CDs with perfect lattice defects can be obtained after dehydration and carbonization, and they have the same strong photobleaching properties as quantum dots. Our work gives an insight to obtain high-quality N-CDs conforming to different requirements through adjusting the reaction conditions, which will make N-CDs more widely used in practical production and life.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial supports by of the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFC0102700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61671299), Shanghai Science and Technology Grant (16JC1402000), Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (17ZR1414100), the Program of Shanghai Academic/Technology Research Leader (15XD1525200), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning (GZ2016005), and Medical-Engineering Crossover Fund of Shanghai Jiao Tong University (YG2016MS26). We also acknowledge analysis support from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Shanghai Jiao Tong University and the Center for Advanced Electronic Materials and Devices of Shanghai Jiao Tong University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.05.036.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical approaches toward graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in energy-related areas
Small
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Chem. Soc. Rev.
One-pot liquid-phase exfoliation from graphite to graphene with carbon quantum dots
Nanoscale
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescent mechanism
Adv. Mater.
